Defendants Magic Gourmet Trading Inc Fung Ping Chen Ng Willy Ng And Guanmao Chens Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Good Faith Settlement Determination Declaration Of Scott S Blackstone And Proposed Order
Off Calendar Aug-05-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Garet O'Keefe, Esq. 510-282-0319 of O'Keefe & O'Keefe for Plaintiff
Michael Descalso, Esq. 415-262-8563 Michael.Descalso@lewisbrisbois.com of Lewis Brisbois for
Defendant
Scott Blackstone, Esq. for Defendant Willy Ng
Gigi Knudtson, Esq. 916-797-4300 gknudtson@knfirm.com of Knudtson & Associates for Defendant Guanmao Chen
Aaron Hancock, Esq. 510-596-0888 for Defendant Willy Ng et al.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT GUANMAO CHEN, Magic Gourmet Trading Inc Fung Ping Chen Ng Willy Ng And Guanmao Chens' Motion For A Good Faith Settlement Determination.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendants Magic Gourmet Trading, Inc., Fung Ping Chen Ng, Willy Ng, and Guanmao Chen's motion to determine good faith settlement is granted. The party asserting the lack of good faith has the burden of proof on that issue (Code Civ. Proc. Sec 877.6(d)). In this case, objector Koi Palace fails to meet its burden and the settlors have provided substantial evidence that the settlement is in the ballpark. (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488; Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1348, 1350 at n.6 [substantial evidence necessary to find that settlement is in good faith].)

In Tech-Bilt, Inc., 38 Cal.3d at 499, the California Supreme Court held the following factors are relevant in evaluating the good faith of a settlement: 1) a rough approximation of plaintiffs' total recovery and the settlor's proportionate liability; 2) the amount paid in settlement; 3) the allocation of settlement proceeds among plaintiffs; 4) a recognition that a settlor should pay less in settlement than he would if he were found liable after a trial; 5) the financial conditions of the settling defendants, as well as the existence of collusion, fraud, or tortious conduct aimed to injure the interests of non-settling defendants; and 6) defendant's settlement figure must not be grossly disproportionate to what a reasonable person, at the time of the settlement, would estimate the settling defendant's liability to be.

Here, Koi Palace asserts that plaintiff incurred $811,872 in past medical damages and could recover $5 million. (Opposition, 4:22-25). The $3.9 million settlement is in good faith given these damages, settlors' proportionate fault (which is high), and the uncertainty regarding plaintiff's life expectancy. The allocation to plaintiff's wife of $400,000 is also reasonable. There is no evidence of collusion and settlors are paying a large percentage of their available insurance. The settlement is in good faith given the totality of the circumstances.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
